title: Patching a cigarette burn in a veneer tabletop
subtitle: Repair a burn or scorch mark in a veneered surface by cutting out the damaged patch, fitting a replacement veneer, and blending the finish
excerpt: How to repair a cigarette burn or scorch mark in a veneered tabletop. Covers cutting out the damaged veneer patch, sourcing or cutting a replacement, gluing in the patch, and blending the finish with colour-matched stopping and wax.
sourceNotes: Practical Furniture Restoration, Julian Worthington (2004). City and Guilds Level 3 Furniture Restoration. UK furniture restoration trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A furniture restoration technique in which a damaged area of veneer is cut out as a geometric patch (diamond, rectangle, or boat-shaped) and replaced with a matching veneer piece cut to exactly the same shape. The geometric shape gives clean, straight edges that are easier to close invisibly than an organic-shape cut following the grain. Boat-shaped patches (elongated ellipses with pointed ends) are the preferred trade shape because the pointed ends are hidden by the grain lines of the surrounding veneer when the patch is oriented with the grain.

content.text: A burn or scorch in a veneer tabletop is repaired by cutting out the damaged patch as a geometric shape and fitting a

. The repair is skilled work: grain matching and colour blending determine whether the patch shows. On antique or figured veneers, a perfect match is very difficult; aim for a result that reads as natural grain variation.

Mark a diamond or boat shape over the burn, oriented so the long axis runs with the grain. The shape must be large enough to include all charred wood. Score through the veneer with the utility knife held against a metal rule, making three or four passes to cut fully through without pressing into the substrate. Lift the cut patch by pressing the thin blade of the chisel under the veneer at one end, working gently to avoid splitting the surrounding veneer.

The cut-out patch is the template. Lay it over a piece of matching veneer and align the grain as closely as possible. Trace around it and cut slightly large with the utility knife. Test-fit the patch dry: it should sit snug in the opening with no visible gaps. Trim with the knife or a chisel until the fit is right.

Apply a thin coat of PVA or hide glue to the substrate and the flesh side of the patch. Press the patch in with a flat block and a G-cramp. Use a piece of cling film or waxed paper between the block and the veneer to stop the block bonding to any squeezed-out glue. Allow to cure fully: 4 hours minimum for PVA. Remove the clamp and press the patch surface: it should feel flush and solid.

Sand the patch area with 240-grit wrapped around a small block, working with the grain only. The patch surface should be level with the surrounding veneer. Fill any small residual gap around the patch edges with a colour-matched wax filler crayon from the wax-stick set, pressing firmly into the join line and scraping flush. Re-apply the same finish as the surrounding table surface. For polish, oil, or lacquer: blend the new finish at the patch edges rather than coating the whole panel.
